enrichment.short_description: Bildr was a no-code visual development platform for building full-stack web applications and SaaS products without writing code; the company shut down its service on May 25, 2026.
enrichment.company_size: Small venture-backed startup team (reported roughly 1.5 million dollars raised); ceased operations in 2026
enrichment.seo_title: Bildr Review: No-Code Web App Builder (Now Discontinued)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bildr was a no-code platform for building full-stack web apps and SaaS products. See what it offered, its pricing history, and why it shut down in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bildr was a no-code visual development platform, founded in 2020, that let non-developers design interfaces, wire up logic, connect databases, and publish production web applications and SaaS products without writing code. Founder Mark Magnuson positioned it as a full-stack, all-in-one environment competing with tools such as Webflow, Bubble, and Adalo, with the added ability to publish to custom domains and self-host on enterprise plans. Historical pricing included a free Explore tier for learning the platform with one builder seat and roughly 20,000 data records, a Launch plan around 29 dollars per month for custom domain publishing, a Pro plan around 119 dollars per month with higher bandwidth and data limits, a one-time 999 dollar Studio Pass for unlimited web apps and Web3 templates, and custom Enterprise pricing starting near 415 dollars per month with self-hosting. Bildr had raised approximately 1.5 million dollars in funding according to a Failory interview. In 2026, Bildr announced it was shutting down entirely. Mark Magnuson explained in a farewell letter that the rise of agentic AI development tools, such as Claude Code, removed the exact constraint Bildr was built to solve (needing code to build software) faster and more flexibly than a visual no-code builder could match; he cited an example of a user migrating a months-long Bildr build to a working app in a couple of days using an AI coding agent. Studio access and data export remained available until May 25, 2026, after which the Bildr Studio went offline and all customer data was deleted roughly 30 days later. The company reached out directly to customers with live projects to help them migrate off the platform, generally toward AI-assisted coding tools or other no-code and low-code alternatives. As of mid-2026, bildr.com displays only a thank-you and farewell message; there is no way to sign up, log in, or purchase any plan. Any directory listing or research on Bildr today must treat it as a discontinued, defunct product rather than an active SaaS offering, since none of its historical pricing, plans, or features can currently be purchased or used.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Bildr
paragraphs: Bildr was a no-code visual development platform, founded in 2020, aimed at letting people without programming skills design and ship real, production-grade web applications. It combined a visual interface builder, a logic and workflow editor, and a built-in database in a single unified studio, rather than requiring users to stitch together separate tools.

The platform was positioned for founders, small businesses, and internal teams who wanted to build custom software, including SaaS products, without hiring a development team. It supported publishing to custom domains and included niche capabilities like Web3 application templates for teams experimenting with blockchain-connected front ends.

In 2026, Bildr's founder Mark Magnuson announced the company was shutting down. He explained that the emergence of agentic AI coding tools had removed the barrier to building software faster and more flexibly than Bildr's no-code approach could, making the platform obsolete. Bildr Studio went offline on May 25, 2026, with customer data deleted roughly 30 days after that date.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features (Historical)
paragraphs: While active, Bildr offered a drag-and-drop visual interface builder for designing web app screens, paired with a visual logic and workflow editor that let users define app behavior without writing code. A built-in database handled data records natively within the platform, removing the need for a separate backend service for most use cases.

Teams could publish apps to custom domains without Bildr branding on paid plans, connect to external services through API integrations, and collaborate with multiple builder seats. The Enterprise tier additionally supported self-hosting for organizations that needed to run the platform on their own infrastructure.

A notable niche feature was a set of Web3 application templates, giving teams a starting point for blockchain-connected interfaces without building the front end from scratch. None of these features are usable today, since the underlying service has been shut down and all hosted projects and data have been deleted.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing (Historical, Service Discontinued)
paragraphs: Before shutting down, Bildr offered a free Explore plan for people learning the platform, including one builder seat, 1 GB of bandwidth, and storage for up to 20,000 data records. Paid tiers scaled up from there: a Launch plan around 29 dollars per month (24 dollars per month billed yearly) removed Bildr branding and added custom domain publishing, while a Pro plan around 119 dollars per month (99 dollars per month billed annually) increased bandwidth, data record, and API call limits substantially.

Bildr also sold a one-time 999 dollar Studio Pass granting unlimited web app publishing to a custom domain and access to additional templates, including Web3 templates. An Enterprise tier started around 415 dollars per month and added a self-hosting option along with custom terms.

None of these plans can be purchased or used today. Bildr Studio went offline on May 25, 2026, and all customer data was deleted about 30 days afterward, so this pricing information is preserved here strictly as historical reference for anyone researching what Bildr used to offer.

features.title: Custom Domains and Branding
features.description: Paid plans allowed publishing apps to a custom domain without Bildr branding.

features.title: API and Integration Support
features.description: Connected to external services and data sources through API calls.

features.title: Web3 App Templates
features.description: Starter templates for building blockchain-connected application interfaces.

features.title: Self-Hosting Option (Enterprise)
features.description: Enterprise customers could deploy Bildr-built applications on their own infrastructure.

features.title: Team Collaboration Seats
features.description: Multiple builder seats let small teams work together inside a shared project.

pros: Enabled non-developers to build full-stack web applications without writing code
Combined visual UI design, logic, and a database in a single unified platform
Offered a genuinely usable free tier for learning and prototyping before it shut down
Provided a data export option and direct migration outreach when the company wound down
Included niche capabilities like Web3 templates and a self-hosting option for enterprise customers
cons: The company shut down entirely on May 25, 2026, so the product is no longer available to new or existing users
All hosted projects and customer data were deleted roughly 30 days after the shutdown date
Former users had to migrate to other platforms or rebuild applications elsewhere with no ongoing support
As a visual no-code builder, it carried the typical flexibility and performance trade-offs of that category while active
Historic plans, including the one-time Studio Pass, are no longer purchasable or usable in any capacity

faqs.question: Is Bildr still available?
faqs.answer: No. Bildr shut down its service, with Studio access ending on May 25, 2026 and all customer data deleted about 30 days later.

faqs.question: Why did Bildr shut down?
faqs.answer: Founder Mark Magnuson said the rise of agentic AI development tools removed the barrier Bildr was built to solve faster than the platform could keep up with, making it obsolete.

faqs.question: What was Bildr used for?
faqs.answer: It was a no-code visual development platform for designing interfaces, building logic, connecting a database, and publishing full-stack web applications and SaaS products.

faqs.question: Can I still export my Bildr project?
faqs.answer: The company offered an export option in the Studio header before the shutdown deadline, but this is no longer available now that the service has closed.

faqs.question: How much did Bildr cost when it was active?
faqs.answer: Plans ranged from a free Explore tier to a Launch plan around 29 dollars per month, a Pro plan around 119 dollars per month, a one-time 999 dollar Studio Pass, and custom Enterprise pricing from about 415 dollars per month.

faqs.question: When was Bildr founded?
faqs.answer: Bildr was founded in 2020.

faqs.question: What should former Bildr users use instead?
faqs.answer: The company pointed departing customers toward AI-assisted coding tools and other no-code or low-code platforms to rebuild their applications.

faqs.question: Did Bildr support self-hosting?
faqs.answer: Yes, its Enterprise tier included a self-hosting option prior to the shutdown.

enrichment.short_description: Binder, hosted at mybinder.org, is a free, open-source service that turns any GitHub repository containing Jupyter notebooks into a live, executable, shareable environment in the browser.
enrichment.company_size: Volunteer-run open-source community project, not a commercial company
enrichment.headquarters: No corporate headquarters; the Binder Project is part of Project Jupyter, which is fiscally sponsored by NumFOCUS, a nonprofit based in Austin, Texas, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Binder (mybinder.org): Free Live Jupyter Notebook Sharing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Binder turns GitHub repos with Jupyter notebooks into free, live, shareable, executable environments in your browser. Learn how mybinder.org works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Binder, publicly hosted at mybinder.org, is a free, open-source service that converts any public GitHub repository containing Jupyter notebooks into a live, interactive, executable computational environment accessible directly in a web browser, with no installation required. It is built on two core open-source components: BinderHub, which deploys the Binder service in the cloud, and repo2docker, which generates reproducible Docker images from the contents of a git repository. Under the hood, mybinder.org is one public deployment within a broader federation of BinderHub instances maintained by the volunteer Binder community. Binder was first announced in 2016 as a way to make research code universally accessible and reproducible; the project merged into the Project Jupyter ecosystem in 2017, and the public mybinder.org service has operated continuously as a free offering since early 2018. Project Jupyter itself is a non-profit, open-source project that grew out of the IPython Project in 2014 and is fiscally sponsored by NumFOCUS, a registered 501c3 nonprofit; there is no commercial company behind Binder, and it is run and maintained by an open community of volunteer contributors rather than paid staff. Because a repository owner only needs to add a dependency file such as requirements.txt or environment.yml, Binder is widely used to attach a one-click, runnable version of code to academic papers, conference talks, teaching materials, and open-source project documentation, supporting any Jupyter kernel including Python, R, and Julia. The service is entirely free, requires no signup or account, and is funded through donated cloud infrastructure and community support rather than subscription revenue. Its main limitations reflect its nonprofit, best-effort nature: sessions are ephemeral and unauthenticated, meaning any files created during a session are lost once it times out unless manually downloaded; there is no persistent storage between sessions; compute resources are limited and not intended for heavy or production workloads; and build or launch times can be slow for large or complex repositories. Because BinderHub and repo2docker are fully open source, institutions and organizations can also deploy their own private BinderHub instance if they need more capacity, persistence, or control than the shared public mybinder.org service provides.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Binder
paragraphs: Binder is a free, open-source service, publicly hosted at mybinder.org, that turns a GitHub repository containing Jupyter notebooks into a live, interactive computing environment that runs entirely in the browser. There is nothing to install locally; a user simply points Binder at a repository, and it builds and launches a runnable copy of that environment on shared cloud infrastructure.

It is powered by two open-source tools built by the Jupyter community: repo2docker, which converts a git repository into a reproducible Docker image based on its dependency files, and BinderHub, which manages building and launching those images on demand. The public mybinder.org deployment is one node in a federation of BinderHub instances run by the volunteer Binder community.

Binder is a sub-project of Project Jupyter, a non-profit, open-source project fiscally sponsored by NumFOCUS. It is not a commercial company; it has no headquarters, sales team, or paid pricing tiers, and it is built, led, and supported entirely by an open community of contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core feature of Binder is turning any public repository with Jupyter notebooks into a shareable link that launches a live, executable version of that environment for anyone who clicks it, with no local setup required. This makes it popular for attaching runnable code to research papers, teaching materials, blog posts, and open-source project READMEs.

Binder supports any language with a Jupyter kernel, not just Python, including R and Julia, as long as the repository specifies the right dependency file. Because the underlying BinderHub and repo2docker stack is fully open source, organizations can also self-host their own private Binder deployment for more control, capacity, or persistence than the free public service offers.

Sessions on the public mybinder.org service are temporary by design: there is no persistent storage, and unsaved work is lost when a session times out, so it is best suited to demos, teaching, and reproducible research rather than ongoing or production data work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Binder, as offered at mybinder.org, is completely free to use. There is no signup, no account, no credit card, and no paid tier of any kind for the public service.

This is possible because Binder is not a commercial product; it is a nonprofit, community-run project under Project Jupyter and fiscally sponsored by NumFOCUS, supported by donated cloud infrastructure and volunteer maintenance rather than subscription revenue.

Organizations that need guaranteed capacity, persistent storage, or private access beyond what the shared public instance offers can deploy and pay for their own self-hosted BinderHub, since the underlying software is open source, but this is a separate, self-managed cost outside of Binder itself.

features.description: Point Binder at any public GitHub repository with a dependency file and it builds a runnable environment automatically.

features.title: Shareable Links

features.title: Federation of BinderHub Deployments

features.title: Free and Fully Open Source

features.title: Popular for Reproducible Research
features.description: Widely used in academic and scientific publishing to attach runnable code environments to papers and talks.

pros: Completely free to use with no account or signup required
Makes any GitHub repository with notebooks instantly interactive and shareable via a single link
Backed by the nonprofit Project Jupyter and NumFOCUS community rather than a commercial vendor with paid tiers
Fully open-source stack that institutions can self-host via their own BinderHub deployment
Widely adopted in academic research, teaching, and conference demos for reproducibility
cons: Sessions are temporary, and unsaved data is lost when a session times out
No persistent storage between sessions, so created files must be downloaded manually
Build and launch times can be slow for large repositories or complex environments
Compute resources are limited and not intended for heavy or production workloads
Relies on donated infrastructure and volunteer maintenance, so uptime is not guaranteed at an enterprise service level

faqs.question: Is Binder really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, mybinder.org is a free public service with no signup or payment required.

faqs.question: Who runs mybinder.org?
faqs.answer: It is run by the volunteer Binder community as part of Project Jupyter, a non-profit project fiscally sponsored by NumFOCUS.

faqs.question: What do I need to use Binder?
faqs.answer: A public GitHub (or similar) repository containing Jupyter notebooks and a dependency file such as requirements.txt or environment.yml.

faqs.question: Does Binder save my changes?
faqs.answer: No, by default Binder sessions are temporary. Any changes are lost once the session ends unless you download the files yourself.

faqs.question: Can I use languages other than Python?
faqs.answer: Yes, Binder supports any Jupyter kernel, including R and Julia, as long as the repository is configured for it.

faqs.question: Can I self-host my own Binder?
faqs.answer: Yes, since BinderHub is open source, organizations can deploy their own private BinderHub instance.

faqs.question: How long has Binder existed?
faqs.answer: Binder was first announced in 2016, joined the Jupyter ecosystem in 2017, and the public mybinder.org service has run since early 2018.

faqs.question: Is Binder suitable for production workloads?
faqs.answer: No, it is designed for demos, teaching, and reproducible research rather than production or heavy compute.

use_cases.title: Reproducible Academic Research
use_cases.description: Researchers attach a Binder link to a paper or GitHub repository so readers can rerun the exact analysis in a live notebook without installing anything.

use_cases.title: Teaching and Workshops
use_cases.description: Instructors share a single Binder link so students can immediately run course notebooks in the browser during a class or workshop.

use_cases.title: Open-Source Project Demos
use_cases.description: Maintainers add a Binder badge to a README so users can try a library or tool interactively before installing it locally.

enrichment.short_description: BinShare.net is a free online tool for pasting and sharing code or text snippets with syntax highlighting, shareable links, and image export, similar to Pastebin.
enrichment.seo_title: BinShare.net: Free Online Code and Text Paste Sharing Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BinShare.net lets you paste and share code or text snippets with syntax highlighting, shareable links, and configurable expiration, free and without signup.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BinShare.net is a free online paste and code-sharing tool that lets users store and share blocks of text or code without needing to sign up for an account. It is positioned as a simpler, faster alternative to copy-pasting large blocks of code directly into chat apps or forums, instead giving users a single short link or a shareable image of their formatted snippet. The tool supports syntax highlighting for a range of common programming and markup languages, including JSON, HTML, CSS, Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, JSX, TSX, Python, PHP, SQL, Dart, and plain text, so shared code renders with proper formatting and color coding for readability. Users can create a paste through a simple New Paste interface, customize the layout, and then share it either as a direct link or as an exported image, which is useful on platforms that do not render code blocks natively. BinShare.net also offers configurable retention settings, letting users choose whether a paste expires after one day, one week, one month, one year, or never, giving some control over how long shared content stays accessible online. The site does not publicly disclose information about its founding date, company or team behind it, headquarters, or funding, and it does not present itself as a commercial company with enterprise tiers; it functions purely as a lightweight, free utility website. It sits in the same category as tools like Pastebin and Codeshare, though it does not appear to offer real-time collaborative editing the way some code-sharing tools do, focusing instead on quick, one-way paste creation and sharing. There is no visible API documentation, team or private workspace functionality, or access control system, so it is best suited for quick, low-stakes sharing of code or text rather than for sensitive or confidential material. Overall, BinShare.net is a narrowly scoped, free developer utility aimed at making it easy to share formatted code snippets via a link or image without any signup friction.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is BinShare.net
paragraphs: BinShare.net is a free online paste and code-sharing website that lets users store text or code snippets and share them via a link or an exported image. It is designed as a lightweight alternative to pasting large blocks of code directly into a chat window or forum post.

The tool requires no signup or account creation; users can open the site, paste their content into a New Paste form, and immediately generate a shareable result. This makes it convenient for quick, one-off sharing during troubleshooting, code reviews, or casual collaboration.

Unlike some code-sharing tools that emphasize live collaborative editing, BinShare.net focuses on straightforward, one-way paste creation, formatting, and sharing, positioning it alongside tools like Pastebin in the broader code-snippet-sharing category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: BinShare.net's core feature is syntax highlighting across a range of common languages, including JSON, HTML, CSS, Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, JSX, TSX, Python, PHP, SQL, Dart, and plain text, so shared snippets are readable and properly formatted rather than appearing as unstyled text.

Beyond generating a shareable link, the tool can export a paste as a formatted image, which is useful for sharing code on platforms that strip formatting or do not render code blocks, such as many social media sites and messaging apps.

Users can also set an expiration window for each paste, choosing between one day, one week, one month, one year, or never, which gives some control over how long the shared content remains publicly accessible.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: BinShare.net is free to use. There is no paid tier, subscription, or account requirement described on the site; users can create and share pastes without any cost.

Because the site does not publish company or ownership information, there are no publicly listed enterprise or team pricing plans, private workspace options, or service-level guarantees beyond the free public tool.
This makes BinShare.net best suited for casual, ad hoc sharing rather than as a paid, contractually supported enterprise code-sharing solution.

features.title: Configurable Expiration
features.description: Choose retention windows of one day, one week, one month, one year, or never.

features.title: No Signup Required
features.description: Create and share a paste without creating an account.

features.title: Clean, Distraction-Free Viewer
features.description: Shared pastes open in a simple, formatted reading view.

features.title: Free to Use
features.description: The core paste and share functionality carries no listed cost.

pros: Free to use with no account or signup needed
Supports syntax highlighting across a wide range of common programming languages
Offers flexible expiration windows, including a permanent never-expire option
Can export a snippet as an image, useful for sharing on platforms that do not render code blocks
Simple, fast interface focused on a single task
cons: Limited public information about the company or team behind the site
No visible enterprise features such as team accounts, private workspaces, or access controls
As a free hosted paste tool, availability and long-term retention are not contractually guaranteed
Fewer collaboration features, such as live co-editing, compared to dedicated tools like Codeshare
No published API documentation for programmatic paste creation

faqs.question: Is BinShare.net free?
faqs.answer: Yes, creating and sharing pastes is free and does not require signup.

faqs.question: What languages does BinShare.net support for syntax highlighting?
faqs.answer: It supports JSON, HTML, CSS, Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, JSX, TSX, Python, PHP, SQL, Dart, and plain text.

faqs.question: Can I control how long my paste stays online?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can set an expiration of one day, one week, one month, one year, or never.

faqs.question: Do I need an account to use BinShare.net?
faqs.answer: No, pastes can be created and shared without registering.

faqs.question: Can I share a snippet as an image instead of a link?
faqs.answer: Yes, BinShare.net supports exporting a formatted snippet as an image.

faqs.question: Is BinShare.net similar to Pastebin?
faqs.answer: Yes, it serves a similar purpose to Pastebin and other code-sharing sites, letting users share formatted code snippets via a link.

faqs.question: Who owns or operates BinShare.net?
faqs.answer: The site does not publicly disclose company or ownership information.

faqs.question: Is BinShare.net suitable for private or sensitive code?
faqs.answer: The site does not publish details on private workspaces or access controls, so sensitive code should be shared with caution.

enrichment.short_description: Binti is cloud-based case management software, a CCWIS, that helps foster care, adoption, and child welfare agencies manage intake, licensing, placements, and eligibility.
enrichment.company_size: Growing venture-backed company serving 300 plus agencies; exact employee count not publicly disclosed
enrichment.seo_title: Binti Review: Foster Care and Child Welfare Case Management
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Binti is cloud case management software for child welfare agencies, covering intake, licensing, placements, and eligibility. See features, pricing, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Binti is a cloud-based, modular case management platform, known as a Comprehensive Child Welfare Information System (CCWIS), built specifically for foster care, adoption, and child welfare agencies. It was founded in 2015 by Felicia Curcuru and Gabe Kopley, with Curcuru serving as CEO, and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Binti's mission is explicitly framed around helping every child find a safe, loving, and stable family, and the company describes itself as mission-driven technology for the child welfare sector. The product covers the end-to-end child welfare workflow through separate but connectable modules, including Intake, Community Crisis Response (Investigations), Licensing, Placements, Case Management, and Eligibility and Payments, alongside a Family First Prevention Services Module for federal compliance, a Family Finding Module to help locate relatives and kin, and dedicated portals for youth in care and CASAs (court-appointed special advocates). Agencies can purchase individual modules or bundle them together, paying through an all-inclusive annual subscription that includes unlimited users, configuration services, and a live chat help desk for both agency staff and families. Binti reports that it has scaled to serve more than 300 to 450 child welfare agencies across roughly 33 to 34 US states plus the District of Columbia, including a number of statewide implementations, with over 12,000 social workers using Binti as their primary daily workspace and the platform touching over 30 percent of children in the US child welfare system according to company figures. Reported outcomes include an average 30 percent increase in the number of quality foster families licensed in an agency's first year, using 18 percent less time, and up to a 40 percent reduction in social workers' administrative time. Binti has raised institutional venture funding, including a Series A round of 12 million dollars in 2021 and a Series B round of 16 million dollars in 2022, from investors such as Founders Fund, First Round Capital, and Kapor Capital. For public sector procurement, Binti partners with Carahsoft as its public sector distributor, making the software available to state and local agencies through contract vehicles such as NASPO ValuePoint, the National Cooperative Purchasing Alliance, E and I Cooperative Services, and OMNIA Partners. Binti does not publish public list pricing; prospective agencies must contact Binti or Carahsoft directly for a custom quote based on which modules and how many users are needed.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Binti
paragraphs: Binti is a cloud-based case management platform purpose-built for the child welfare sector, technically classified as a Comprehensive Child Welfare Information System (CCWIS). It was founded in 2015 by Felicia Curcuru and Gabe Kopley and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with a stated mission centered on helping every child find a safe, loving, and stable family.

Rather than offering a single monolithic system, Binti is built as a set of connectable modules covering the full child welfare workflow, from initial intake and investigations through licensing, placements, ongoing case management, and eligibility and payment processing. Agencies can start with one module and expand into others as needs and budgets grow.

The platform is used by hundreds of foster care, adoption, and child welfare agencies across the United States, including a number of full statewide implementations, and is backed by institutional venture funding from investors such as Founders Fund and First Round Capital.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Binti's core modules include Intake, Community Crisis Response (Investigations), Licensing, Placements, Case Management, and Eligibility and Payments, each addressing a distinct stage of the child welfare process. Agencies can license individual modules or combine them into a broader implementation.

Beyond the core workflow modules, Binti offers a Family First Prevention Services Module to help agencies track compliance with federal Family First legislation, and a Family Finding Module to support locating and engaging relatives and kin for potential placements. Dedicated portals give youth in care and CASAs their own way to interact with case information.

Every subscription includes unlimited users within the licensed modules, configuration services during onboarding, and ongoing support through a live chat help desk available to both agency staff and the families they serve.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Binti does not publish public list pricing on its website. Instead, agencies purchase access through an all-inclusive annual subscription model, where modules can be bought individually or bundled together depending on the agency's needs and budget.

For government and public sector buyers, Binti is available through its public sector distributor Carahsoft, on established procurement contract vehicles including NASPO ValuePoint, the National Cooperative Purchasing Alliance (NCPA), E and I Cooperative Services, and OMNIA Partners, which simplifies compliance with state and local purchasing rules.

Because pricing depends on which modules an agency selects and how many users and cases are involved, organizations interested in Binti need to request a custom quote directly from Binti or through Carahsoft rather than referencing a fixed public price list.

features.title: Modular CCWIS Platform
features.description: Intake, Licensing, Placements, Case Management, and Eligibility and Payments modules can be purchased individually or bundled.

features.title: Family First Prevention Services Module
features.description: Supports agency compliance with federal Family First prevention services legislation.

features.title: Family Finding Module
features.description: Helps agencies locate and engage relatives and kin for potential placements.

features.title: Youth and CASA Portals
features.description: Dedicated portals for youth in care and court-appointed special advocates.

features.title: Community Crisis Response (Investigations)
features.description: Supports intake and investigation workflows for child welfare reports.

features.title: Unlimited Users per Subscription
features.description: Annual subscriptions include unlimited user seats within an agency's licensed modules.

features.description: Available through Carahsoft on multiple public sector contract vehicles.

features.title: Configuration and Support Services
features.description: Includes onboarding configuration services and a live chat help desk for agency staff and families.

pros: Purpose-built specifically for child welfare, foster care, and adoption agencies rather than generic case management
Modular design lets agencies start with one module and add more over time
Available through established government procurement contract vehicles via Carahsoft
Reports strong outcomes, including a cited 30 percent increase in licensed foster families and up to 40 percent time savings for social workers
Includes dedicated portals for youth and CASAs, not just caseworkers and administrators
Backed by significant venture funding, including Series A and Series B rounds from established investors
cons: Pricing is not publicly listed and requires a custom quote through Binti or Carahsoft
Primarily built for government and agency procurement, which can mean longer sales and implementation cycles
As a specialized CCWIS, it is not designed for general-purpose case management outside child welfare
Full feature set is spread across multiple modules, which may require budgeting for several purchases over time
Best suited to US state and local child welfare agencies rather than international or private-sector use

faqs.question: What is Binti used for?
faqs.answer: Binti is case management software used by foster care, adoption, and child welfare agencies to manage intake, licensing, placements, case work, and eligibility.

faqs.question: When was Binti founded?
faqs.answer: Binti was founded in 2015 by Felicia Curcuru and Gabe Kopley.

faqs.question: How much does Binti cost?
faqs.answer: Binti does not publish public pricing. Agencies purchase modules individually or bundled through custom annual subscriptions, available directly or via Carahsoft.

faqs.question: How many agencies use Binti?
faqs.answer: Binti serves several hundred child welfare agencies across dozens of US states and the District of Columbia, including a number of statewide implementations.

faqs.question: Is Binti available to government agencies through procurement contracts?
faqs.answer: Yes, Binti is offered as a public sector solution through Carahsoft on multiple contract vehicles including NASPO ValuePoint and NCPA.

faqs.question: What is a CCWIS?
faqs.answer: CCWIS stands for Comprehensive Child Welfare Information System, the federal designation for modern, modular child welfare case management systems like Binti.

faqs.question: Does Binti include a portal for foster youth?
faqs.answer: Yes, Binti includes a dedicated Youth portal as well as a portal for CASAs (court-appointed special advocates).

faqs.question: Who are Binti's investors?
faqs.answer: Binti has raised venture funding including a Series A and Series B round, with investors such as Founders Fund, First Round Capital, and Kapor Capital.

use_cases.title: Foster Parent Licensing
use_cases.description: Agencies use Binti's Licensing module to move foster parent applicants through background checks, home studies, and approval workflows online.
use_cases.audience: Foster care licensing staff at child welfare agencies

use_cases.title: Statewide Child Welfare Case Management
use_cases.description: State child welfare departments deploy Binti agency-wide to standardize intake, placements, and case management for children in care.
use_cases.audience: State and county child welfare departments

use_cases.title: Family First Prevention Compliance
use_cases.description: Agencies use the Family First Prevention Services Module to track prevention services and remain compliant with federal Family First legislation.

features: Live chat support for staff and families

enrichment.short_description: Birst is a cloud business intelligence and analytics platform originally founded in 2004; Infor acquired it in 2017, and it now operates as Infor Birst, a product line inside Infor's suite rather than a standalone company.
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 260 employees at the time of Infor's 2017 acquisition; now a product line within Infor, a much larger enterprise software company
enrichment.headquarters: Originally San Francisco, California; now operated as part of Infor, headquartered in New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Birst (Infor Birst) Review: Cloud BI and Analytics Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Birst is a cloud BI platform now owned by Infor and sold as Infor Birst. See its history, features, pricing model, and place in Infor's product suite.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Birst is a cloud-native business intelligence and analytics platform originally founded in 2004 and headquartered in San Francisco, California. It was an early pioneer of cloud, multi-tenant BI architecture, known for its Networked BI approach that let organizations blend centralized, IT-governed data models with decentralized, business-user-driven analytics on the same platform. On May 31, 2017, Infor, a large enterprise software company, acquired Birst for 75 million dollars net of cash acquired; at the time of acquisition, Birst had approximately 260 employees and more than 300 customers worldwide. Following the acquisition, Birst was absorbed into Infor's product portfolio and is now sold exclusively under the name Infor Birst, positioned within Infor's Data and Insights category as part of the broader Infor Industry Cloud Platform. Birst does not exist today as a standalone, independently operated company or brand; it is one analytics product line within Infor, which is itself headquartered in New York, New York. Infor continues to actively develop and document the product, with user and administration documentation published for current release versions, indicating ongoing investment rather than a sunset. Infor Birst emphasizes automated data preparation and modeling using patented automation and machine learning techniques designed to reduce the manual work traditionally required to build BI data models, along with self-service dashboards, a unified semantic layer across multiple data sources, and AI-infused analytics capabilities aimed at helping organizations make faster, more informed decisions. The platform integrates especially deeply with Infor's own CloudSuite and other Infor ERP products, making it a natural analytics layer for existing Infor customers, though it can also be deployed as a more general-purpose BI tool. Infor offers a free trial of Infor Birst that does not require credit card details up front. Public list pricing is not published by Infor; the product is sold on a custom, quote-based basis, and third-party research and pricing-estimate sites report figures in the range of roughly 2,000 to 2,500 dollars per month at the low end, scaling up substantially with user count, though these figures are third-party estimates rather than confirmed vendor pricing. Competitively, Infor Birst sits alongside major self-service BI platforms such as Microsoft Power BI, Tableau, and Looker, though its differentiation today comes largely from its native fit within the Infor ecosystem rather than as a pure best-of-breed standalone BI product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Birst (Infor Birst)
paragraphs: Birst is a cloud business intelligence and analytics platform that was founded in 2004 in San Francisco, California, and became known as one of the early pioneers of cloud-native, multi-tenant BI, with an architecture the company called Networked BI that blended centralized and decentralized analytics on one platform.

In 2017, Infor, a large enterprise software company, acquired Birst for 75 million dollars. Since that acquisition, Birst has not operated as an independent company; it is sold and marketed exclusively as Infor Birst, a product within Infor's Data and Insights category inside the broader Infor Industry Cloud Platform.

Infor continues to actively maintain and update the product, publishing documentation for current release versions, so while Birst is no longer a standalone brand, Infor Birst remains a live, supported analytics product rather than a discontinued one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Infor Birst emphasizes automated data preparation and modeling, using patented automation and machine learning techniques originally developed by Birst to reduce the manual effort typically required to build and maintain BI data models across multiple source systems.

The platform provides self-service dashboards and reporting so business users can explore data and build their own visualizations without deep technical training, on top of a unified semantic layer that consolidates data from many different systems into consistent, governed definitions.

Infor Birst is particularly deeply integrated with Infor's own CloudSuite and other Infor ERP products, making it a natural default analytics layer for organizations already running Infor systems, while still supporting a broad range of external data source connections for organizations that use it as a more general BI tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Infor does not publish public list pricing for Infor Birst; the product is sold on a custom, quote-based basis tailored to the number of users, data volume, and modules an organization needs.
Infor does offer a free trial of Infor Birst that does not require credit card information, giving prospective customers a way to evaluate the platform before committing to a paid contract.

Third-party pricing research and cost-estimate sites report figures that can start in the low thousands of dollars per month and scale up significantly with user count and complexity, but these are third-party estimates rather than figures officially confirmed by Infor, so organizations should request a direct quote for accurate pricing.

features.description: Applies automation and machine learning to speed up analysis and pattern detection.

features.title: Broad Data Source Integration
features.description: Connects to numerous databases, cloud applications, and Infor ERP systems.

features.title: Networked BI Architecture
features.description: Supports both centralized, IT-managed and decentralized, business-user analytics on one platform.

features.title: Deep Infor Ecosystem Integration
features.description: Tightly integrated with Infor CloudSuite and other Infor ERP products.

features.title: Free Trial and Enterprise Support
features.description: Offers a no-credit-card free trial alongside enterprise-level implementation support.

pros: Long track record as one of the original cloud-native BI platforms, dating back to 2004
Strong automation for data preparation and modeling reduces manual data engineering work
Deep, native integration with Infor's ERP and CloudSuite products for existing Infor customers
Offers a free trial without requiring credit card details
Backed by Infor's larger enterprise resources and continued, active product development
cons: No longer operates as an independent company or standalone brand; it is one product line within Infor's broader suite
Pricing is not publicly listed and requires a custom quote from Infor
Best-fit value is strongest for organizations already using Infor ERP products rather than as a pure best-of-breed standalone BI tool
Faces intense competition from dedicated BI leaders such as Power BI, Tableau, and Looker
Third-party estimates suggest enterprise costs can scale into the thousands of dollars per month, though Infor does not publish these figures officially

faqs.question: Is Birst still an independent company?
faqs.answer: No. Birst was acquired by Infor in 2017 for 75 million dollars and now operates as Infor Birst, a product within Infor's Data and Insights suite rather than a separate company.

faqs.question: When was Birst founded?
faqs.answer: Birst was founded in 2004 and was originally headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: What is Infor Birst used for?
faqs.answer: It is a cloud business intelligence and analytics platform used for data modeling, dashboards, self-service reporting, and AI-assisted analytics.

faqs.question: How much does Infor Birst cost?
faqs.answer: Infor does not publish public pricing for Birst; it is sold on a custom quote basis, and third-party estimates suggest enterprise costs can run from roughly two thousand dollars per month upward depending on user count.

faqs.question: Does Infor Birst offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Infor offers a free trial that does not require credit card details.

faqs.question: Is Infor Birst only for Infor ERP customers?
faqs.answer: No, it can be used as a standalone BI platform, but it has particularly deep integration with Infor CloudSuite and other Infor ERP products.

faqs.question: Is Birst actively maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, Infor continues to develop and document the product, with release documentation available for current product versions.

faqs.question: What was Birst known for before the Infor acquisition?
faqs.answer: It was known as a pioneer of cloud-native, multi-tenant business intelligence with a Networked BI architecture supporting both centralized and decentralized analytics.

enrichment.short_description: Bismuth was a free, open-source tiling window manager add-on for the KDE Plasma desktop on Linux; the project was archived in June 2024 after KDE Plasma added native tiling support.
enrichment.company_size: Open-source community project maintained by volunteer contributors, not a company
enrichment.seo_title: Bismuth: Free KDE Plasma Tiling Window Manager (Archived)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bismuth was an open-source tiling window manager add-on for KDE Plasma, similar to i3 or dwm. See its features, install steps, and archived status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bismuth was a free, open-source tiling window manager add-on for the KDE Plasma desktop environment on Linux, designed to automatically arrange open windows into tiled layouts and let users manage them via keyboard shortcuts, in a style similar to i3, Sway, or dwm. It was created as a fork of an earlier project called Krohnkite, after Krohnkite's original maintainer had been inactive for more than a year; Bismuth carried the same core tiling functionality forward while adding its own configuration module and additional features. The project integrated natively with KDE Plasma features such as Virtual Desktops and Activities, supported multiple switchable tiling layouts for different workflows, and included a custom border-only window decoration styled for tiling use. It required KDE Plasma 5.20 or later, Qt 5.15 or later, and KDE Frameworks 5.78 or later, and was packaged for installation on major Linux distributions including Ubuntu and Debian (via apt install kwin-bismuth), Fedora (via dnf install bismuth), Arch Linux (via the AUR), and community or official packages on Solus, openSUSE, and Gentoo. The project was hosted on GitHub under the Bismuth-Forge organization, grew to roughly 2,400 GitHub stars and 96 forks, and was supported in part through community donations via Open Collective. In June 2024, specifically on June 16, 2024, the Bismuth-Forge/bismuth repository was archived by its owner and made read-only, effectively ending active development. This followed KDE Plasma's introduction of native, built-in tiling window management starting with Plasma 5.27, which added first-party APIs and a polished tiling user experience directly in the desktop environment, reducing the practical need for a third-party extension like Bismuth. As of 2026, Bismuth is best understood as a historically significant but now-unmaintained open-source project; it remains free and its source code and past distro packages are still publicly available, but it receives no further updates or bug fixes, and users on modern KDE Plasma releases are generally directed toward Plasma's native tiling feature instead of installing Bismuth.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Bismuth
paragraphs: Bismuth was a free, open-source add-on for the KDE Plasma desktop environment on Linux that automatically tiled open windows and let users manage them via keyboard shortcuts, bringing an i3-style or dwm-style tiling workflow to Plasma users without requiring a separate window manager or desktop environment.

The project began as a fork of an earlier tool called Krohnkite, created after Krohnkite's original maintainer had been inactive for over a year, and it was developed and hosted under the Bismuth-Forge organization on GitHub, growing to roughly 2,400 stars and dozens of contributors.

In June 2024, the project's GitHub repository was archived and made read-only, effectively ending active development, largely because KDE Plasma introduced its own native tiling window management starting with Plasma 5.27, reducing the need for a third-party extension.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bismuth's core function was automatic window tiling: rather than manually dragging and resizing windows, users' open windows were arranged into layouts automatically, with keyboard shortcuts available for rearranging, resizing, and switching between windows, similar to tools like i3, Sway, and dwm on other desktop environments.

It integrated with native KDE Plasma features such as Virtual Desktops and Activities, supported multiple switchable layouts so users could adapt tiling behavior to different contexts, and shipped with its own border-only window decoration designed to look and feel like a proper tiling window manager rather than a bolted-on extension.

The project was packaged for most major Linux distributions, including official or community packages for Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, Arch Linux, Solus, openSUSE, and Gentoo, making installation straightforward for KDE Plasma users on a wide range of systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bismuth was entirely free and open source, with no paid tier, license fee, or subscription of any kind; its source code was publicly available on GitHub under an open-source license.
Ongoing development was supported in part by voluntary community donations through Open Collective rather than commercial sales or a company backing the project.

The project is now archived and unmaintained, but its source code and historical distro packages remain publicly available at no cost for users who want to install it on compatible, typically older, Plasma versions.

features.title: Automatic Window Tiling
features.description: Arranges open windows into tiled layouts without manual resizing or dragging.

features.title: Keyboard-Driven Workflow
features.description: Manage and rearrange windows via keyboard shortcuts, similar to i3, Sway, or dwm.

features.title: Native Plasma Integration
features.description: Worked with KDE's Virtual Desktops and Activities features.

features.title: Multiple Layout Options
features.description: Switch between different tiling layouts for different contexts.

features.title: Custom Window Decoration
features.description: Included a border-only window decoration styled for tiling use.

features.title: Broad Distro Packaging
features.description: Available through official or community packages on Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, Arch, Solus, openSUSE, and Gentoo.

features.title: Fully Open Source
features.description: Source code and packaging scripts were publicly available under an open-source license.

pros: Completely free and open source with no cost to install or use
Brought i3-style, keyboard-driven tiling to KDE Plasma users without switching desktop environments
Integrated natively with Plasma features like Virtual Desktops and Activities
Packaged for most major Linux distributions, making installation straightforward
Had an active community with over two thousand GitHub stars and dozens of contributors before archival
cons: The project was archived and made read-only on GitHub in June 2024 and no longer receives updates
Largely unnecessary for most users now that KDE Plasma added native tiling window management starting with Plasma 5.27
Compatibility issues can arise on newer Plasma or KWin versions since the extension is unmaintained
Limited to KDE Plasma only, with no support for other desktop environments
Users seeking ongoing support or bug fixes must now rely on community forks rather than the official project

faqs.question: Is Bismuth still maintained?
faqs.answer: No, the Bismuth-Forge repository was archived and made read-only on GitHub on June 16, 2024.

faqs.question: Why was Bismuth discontinued?
faqs.answer: KDE Plasma introduced native tiling window management starting with Plasma 5.27, which reduced the need for a third-party extension like Bismuth.

faqs.question: What was Bismuth?
faqs.answer: It was a free, open-source KDE Plasma add-on that automatically tiled windows and let users manage them via keyboard, similar to i3, Sway, or dwm.

faqs.question: How do I get tiling on KDE Plasma now?
faqs.answer: Newer versions of Plasma, 5.27 and later, include built-in native tiling, which is the recommended path instead of installing Bismuth.

faqs.question: What was Bismuth based on?
faqs.answer: Bismuth was a fork of an earlier project called Krohnkite, created after Krohnkite's original maintainer was inactive for more than a year.

faqs.question: Was Bismuth free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, it was entirely free and open source, supported in part by community donations through Open Collective.

faqs.question: What versions of Plasma did Bismuth support?
faqs.answer: It required KDE Plasma 5.20 or later, Qt 5.15 or later, and KDE Frameworks 5.78 or later.

faqs.question: Can I still install Bismuth on Linux?
faqs.answer: Existing distro packages may still be installable on older Plasma versions, but the project is unmaintained and may not work correctly on current releases.

use_cases.title: i3-Style Tiling on KDE Plasma
use_cases.description: Linux users who liked the keyboard-driven workflow of i3 or dwm installed Bismuth to get similar tiling behavior without leaving the KDE Plasma desktop.
use_cases.audience: Linux power users and KDE Plasma desktop users

use_cases.title: Multi-Monitor Developer Workflows
use_cases.description: Developers used Bismuth's automatic tiling and layout switching to keep terminals, editors, and browsers organized across multiple monitors.

use_cases.title: Legacy Plasma Systems Without Native Tiling
use_cases.description: Users running Plasma versions older than 5.27, which lack built-in tiling, used Bismuth to add tiling functionality.
use_cases.audience: Users on older KDE Plasma releases

enrichment.short_description: Bitnami, owned by Broadcom via VMware, is a catalog of pre-packaged open-source application containers and Helm charts; since late 2025, most production-grade images require a paid Bitnami Secure Images subscription, with only a small free tier for latest-tag, non-production use.
enrichment.company_size: Originally a small startup (Bitrock); now operated as part of Broadcom, a large multinational technology company
enrichment.headquarters: Originally Seville, Spain (as Bitrock); now operated as part of Broadcom and VMware, with Broadcom headquartered in Palo Alto, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Bitnami Review 2026: What Is Still Free After Broadcom Changes
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bitnami offers pre-packaged open-source app containers and Helm charts. See what changed under Broadcom, what is still free, and Secure Images pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bitnami is a catalog of pre-packaged, ready-to-run open-source application stacks, container images, and Helm charts, historically prized for making software like databases, content management systems, and developer tools instantly deployable. It traces back to 2011, sponsored by a company called Bitrock, founded in Seville, Spain by Daniel Lopez Ridruejo and Erica Brescia, and it received early Y Combinator-backed seed funding. VMware acquired Bitnami (along with related Bitrock assets such as InstallBuilder) on May 15, 2019. VMware itself was later acquired by Broadcom in November 2023, bringing Bitnami under Broadcom's ownership. For most of its history, Bitnami was known for offering hundreds of free, versioned container images and Helm charts that developers and organizations could pull and deploy without cost. That changed significantly starting in 2025: Broadcom announced that, effective around August 28, 2025 (later the full removal of the old public catalog was pushed to around September 29, 2025), it would restructure the Bitnami container catalog into three distinct repositories. The main docker.io/bitnami repository now hosts only a limited set of hardened images available exclusively with latest tags, explicitly intended for development and non-production use, reportedly around 44 images. A new docker.io/bitnamilegacy repository archives all historical image versions but receives no further security updates. A docker.io/bitnamisecure repository provides preview access tied to the paid offering. Production-grade, versioned, and hardened images now require a paid Bitnami Secure Images (also referred to as Bitnami Premium) subscription, which bundles unlimited container pulls, access to versioned and long-term-support images, continuous vulnerability scanning and patching, software bills of materials, VEX documentation, and enterprise support. Third-party reporting cites annual subscription costs in the range of roughly 50,000 to 72,000 dollars, with at least one AWS Marketplace listing around 6,000 dollars per month on a 12-month contract; Broadcom has not published a single official public price list, so these figures should be treated as third-party estimates rather than confirmed vendor pricing. Existing Bitnami Helm charts remain published as OCI artifacts at docker.io/bitnamicharts, but they no longer receive updates and may require overriding bundled image references with currently valid images to deploy successfully. In short, as of 2026 Bitnami is best described as a freemium, largely paywalled service: a genuinely useful but narrow free tier still exists for development and testing, while the broad, free, production-grade catalog that made Bitnami famous no longer exists in its historical form.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bitnami
paragraphs: Bitnami is a catalog of pre-packaged, ready-to-run open-source application stacks, distributed as container images and Helm charts, historically covering hundreds of popular open-source applications such as databases, content management systems, and developer tools. It traces its roots to 2011, sponsored by a Seville, Spain-based company called Bitrock, and was acquired by VMware in 2019.

VMware itself was subsequently acquired by Broadcom in November 2023, which brought Bitnami fully into the Broadcom portfolio. Bitnami is no longer an independent company; it now operates as a product and service line inside Broadcom's much larger enterprise software business.

For most of its existence, Bitnami was known as a source of free, easily deployable, versioned container images and one-click cloud marketplace listings. That reputation changed substantially in 2025, when Broadcom restructured the free catalog and introduced a paid subscription for most production-grade images, a shift developers should understand before relying on Bitnami images in new projects.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Bitnami's catalog covers pre-packaged application containers for a wide range of open-source software, alongside packaged Helm charts for deploying multi-component application stacks on Kubernetes. Historically it also offered one-click deployable listings on cloud marketplaces such as AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

Under the current Broadcom-era structure, the catalog is split into three repositories: a limited free docker.io/bitnami repository with hardened, latest-tag-only images meant for development and non-production use; a docker.io/bitnamilegacy repository that archives historical image versions without further updates; and a docker.io/bitnamisecure repository tied to the paid offering.

Paying subscribers to Bitnami Secure Images (also called Bitnami Premium) get unlimited container pulls, access to versioned and long-term-support image tags, continuous vulnerability scanning and patching, software bills of materials, VEX documentation, and enterprise support, positioning the paid tier as a supply-chain-security product rather than just a convenience catalog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bitnami's free tier today is intentionally narrow: it provides a limited set of hardened container images, reportedly around 44, available only with the latest tag and explicitly positioned for development and non-production use rather than production deployments.

Production-grade, versioned, and hardened images require a paid Bitnami Secure Images or Bitnami Premium subscription. Broadcom has not published a single official public price list, but third-party reporting cites enterprise subscription costs in the range of roughly 50,000 to 72,000 dollars per year, with at least one AWS Marketplace listing priced around 6,000 dollars per month on a 12-month contract.

Existing Bitnami Helm charts remain available as OCI artifacts at docker.io/bitnamicharts at no direct cost to pull, but they no longer receive updates, so teams relying on them for production Kubernetes deployments may need to override the bundled image references with currently maintained, valid images.

features.title: Pre-Packaged Application Containers
features.description: Ready-to-run container images for popular open-source software like databases, CMSs, and developer tools.

features.title: Helm Charts for Kubernetes
features.description: Packaged Helm charts for deploying multi-component application stacks on Kubernetes.

features.title: Hardened, Security-Scanned Images
features.description: Paid Secure Images come with minimized CVEs, software bills of materials, and VEX documentation.

features.title: Cloud Marketplace Listings
features.description: Historically available as one-click deployable images across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud marketplaces.

features.title: Multi-Tier Repository Structure
features.description: Separate repositories for free latest-tag images, legacy archived versions, and paid secure images.

features.title: Continuous Vulnerability Scanning
features.description: Ongoing CVE scans and patched updates included for paid subscribers.

features.title: Enterprise Support
features.description: Included with a Bitnami Secure Images or Bitnami Premium subscription.

features.title: Broad Open-Source Application Catalog
features.description: Historically covered hundreds of applications spanning databases, web servers, and developer tools.

pros: Long-established, well-known catalog covering hundreds of popular open-source applications
Paid Secure Images tier offers genuinely hardened, minimal-CVE images with software bills of materials and enterprise support for compliance-sensitive organizations
A limited free tier still exists for development and non-production experimentation
Helm charts remain accessible as OCI artifacts, easing migration for existing Kubernetes users
Backed by Broadcom's larger infrastructure and security resources
cons: Since 2025, most production-grade, versioned container images are no longer free and require a paid Bitnami Secure Images or Bitnami Premium subscription
Reported paid subscription pricing runs into the tens of thousands of dollars per year, out of reach for many small teams and individual developers
The free tier is restricted to latest-tag-only images explicitly intended for non-production use, so pinned versions are not free
Historical, previously public image versions were moved to a legacy repository that no longer receives updates
The abrupt 2025 policy change disrupted many existing production deployments and Helm chart-based Kubernetes clusters that assumed free, versioned image access

faqs.question: Is Bitnami still free?
faqs.answer: Only partially. A limited free tier remains for development and non-production use, restricted to latest-tag images. Most production-grade, versioned, or hardened images now require a paid Bitnami Secure Images subscription.

faqs.question: When did Bitnami stop being fully free?
faqs.answer: Broadcom announced the change in mid-2025, with the shift taking effect around August 28, 2025 and the old public catalog being largely removed around September 29, 2025.

faqs.question: Who owns Bitnami now?
faqs.answer: Bitnami is owned by Broadcom, having come under Broadcom's ownership through its 2023 acquisition of VMware, which itself had acquired Bitnami in 2019.

faqs.question: How much does a Bitnami Secure Images subscription cost?
faqs.answer: Broadcom does not always publish exact figures, but third-party reporting cites enterprise subscription costs in the tens of thousands of dollars per year, with some marketplace listings around six thousand dollars per month on a twelve-month contract.

faqs.question: What happened to older Bitnami image versions?
faqs.answer: They were moved to a legacy repository, docker.io/bitnamilegacy, that is archived and no longer receives security updates.

faqs.question: Are Bitnami Helm charts still usable?
faqs.answer: The packaged Helm charts remain published as OCI artifacts, but they no longer receive updates, so deployments may need overriding with valid current images.

faqs.question: When was Bitnami founded?
faqs.answer: Bitnami traces back to 2011, sponsored by a company called Bitrock founded in Seville, Spain.

faqs.question: Is Bitnami still useful for individual developers?
faqs.answer: It can still be useful for development and testing through the limited free latest-tag catalog, but production use now generally requires a paid subscription.

use_cases.title: Quick Local Development Environments
use_cases.description: Developers pull a free, latest-tag Bitnami container image to spin up a database or app stack for local testing without configuring it from scratch.

use_cases.title: Enterprise Kubernetes Deployments
use_cases.description: Organizations subscribe to Bitnami Secure Images to get hardened, continuously patched, versioned container images with software bills of materials for compliance-sensitive production Kubernetes clusters.

enrichment.short_description: Bitrise is a mobile-focused CI/CD platform that automates building, testing, and deploying iOS and Android apps for development teams.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Hobby plan); paid plans start around 89 to 99 dollars per month

enrichment.seo_title: Bitrise Review 2026: Mobile CI/CD Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bitrise is a mobile DevOps and CI/CD platform for iOS and Android. See pricing plans, key features, pros, cons, and frequently asked questions.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bitrise is a mobile DevOps platform providing continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) specifically designed around the needs of iOS and Android app development, rather than being a general-purpose CI/CD tool adapted for mobile. It was founded in 2014 by Barnabas Birmacher, Viktor Benei, and Daniel Balla, and is headquartered in Budapest, Hungary, with additional offices in San Francisco, California and London, United Kingdom, operating as a remote-first organization. Bitrise reports serving more than 6,000 customers and over 100,000 developers, processing more than 2 million builds per month on its infrastructure. The platform centers on a visual workflow editor (with an underlying YAML configuration) that lets teams define build, test, and deployment pipelines using a large marketplace of prebuilt integration steps covering tasks like code signing, testing frameworks, and app store submission. It provides both macOS and Linux build machines in multiple performance tiers, since mobile teams need Apple hardware to build and sign iOS apps while Android builds can run on Linux. Additional platform capabilities include build caching to speed up repeat builds, a Release Management add-on for coordinating app store submissions and rollouts, and support for concurrent builds and team role management, with limits varying by plan. Bitrise's pricing follows a freemium model: the free Hobby plan includes one private app, up to five concurrent builds, a 90-minute build timeout, and 300 credits per month with community support. Paid plans scale from a Starter tier around 89 to 99 dollars per month (with up to three concurrencies, ten team members, and two private apps) to a Pro tier around 200 to 218 dollars per month (with higher concurrency across macOS and Linux machines, unlimited team members, and unlimited private apps), and a custom Enterprise tier for high-volume teams offering dedicated or private cloud options and even self-hosted AWS deployment capability, with third-party reporting suggesting Enterprise pricing can start around 2,000 dollars per month for very high build volumes. Some capabilities, such as advanced Build Cache tiers and Release Management, are priced as separate add-ons on top of the base subscription rather than being bundled in automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Bitrise
paragraphs: Bitrise is a mobile DevOps platform that provides continuous integration and continuous delivery specifically built around the workflows of iOS and Android app development. Rather than adapting a general-purpose CI/CD tool for mobile, it was designed from the ground up to handle mobile-specific needs like code signing, provisioning profiles, and app store submissions.

The company was founded in 2014 by Barnabas Birmacher, Viktor Benei, and Daniel Balla, and is headquartered in Budapest, Hungary, with additional offices in San Francisco and London as part of a remote-first organizational structure.

Bitrise reports serving more than 6,000 customers and over 100,000 developers, with more than 2 million builds processed on its infrastructure each month, making it one of the more established players specifically in the mobile CI/CD category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Bitrise is a visual workflow editor, backed by an underlying YAML configuration, that teams use to assemble build, test, and deployment pipelines from a large marketplace of prebuilt integration steps covering testing frameworks, code signing, and app store deployment tasks.

Because mobile builds require different hardware for different platforms, Bitrise offers both macOS build machines (needed to build and sign iOS apps) and Linux build machines (for Android builds), available in multiple performance tiers depending on the plan.

Additional capabilities include build caching to speed up repeat builds by reusing dependencies and artifacts, a Release Management add-on to coordinate app store submissions and rollouts from within the platform, and support for running multiple builds concurrently, with concurrency limits and team member counts varying by subscription tier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Bitrise offers a free Hobby plan that includes one private app, up to five concurrent builds, a 90-minute build timeout, and 300 credits per month with community-level support, making it usable for individual developers or small side projects.

Paid plans start with Starter at roughly 89 to 99 dollars per month depending on monthly or annual billing, offering up to three concurrencies, up to ten team members, two private apps, and access to medium-sized Linux and Apple silicon build machines. The Pro plan, around 200 to 218 dollars per month, raises concurrency limits substantially, removes the team member and private app caps, and adds access to larger build machines.

Large organizations can move to a custom Enterprise plan offering flexible concurrency, unlimited team members, longer build timeouts of up to four hours, and dedicated, private cloud, or self-hosted AWS deployment options; third-party reporting suggests Enterprise pricing can start around 2,000 dollars per month for high build volumes. Some features, including advanced Build Cache tiers and Release Management beyond the basic level, are billed as separate add-ons on top of the base plan.

features.title: Mobile-Specific CI/CD Pipelines
features.description: Purpose-built workflows for building, testing, and deploying iOS and Android apps.

features.title: Visual Workflow Editor
features.description: Configure build pipelines via a visual editor or underlying YAML configuration.

features.title: Steps Marketplace
features.description: Hundreds of prebuilt community and official integration steps for testing, code signing, and deployment.

features.title: macOS and Linux Build Machines
features.description: Access to Apple silicon and Linux virtual machines sized for different build workloads.

features.title: Build Caching
features.description: Speeds up repeat builds by caching dependencies and build artifacts.

features.title: Release Management Add-On
features.description: Manages app store submissions and release rollouts from within the platform.

features.title: Concurrent Builds and Team Roles
features.description: Supports multiple simultaneous builds and team member management depending on plan.

features.title: Enterprise and Self-Hosted Options
features.description: Dedicated or private cloud deployment, including self-hosting on AWS for large organizations.

pros: Purpose-built for mobile CI/CD rather than a general-purpose pipeline retrofitted for mobile
Large marketplace of integration steps covering testing, code signing, and app store deployment
Offers a genuinely usable free Hobby tier for individual developers and small projects
Supports both macOS and Linux build machines, including different machine sizes for performance needs
Enterprise tier offers dedicated, private cloud, and self-hosted deployment options
Backed by a large, established customer base and remote-first team with offices across three countries
cons: Paid plans can become expensive quickly for teams needing higher concurrency or macOS build minutes
Some valuable features, like advanced Build Cache and Release Management tiers, are separately priced add-ons
Free Hobby tier is limited to a single private app and modest monthly credits
Pricing structure with multiple tiers and add-ons can be complex to estimate total cost upfront
Enterprise pricing is custom and not transparently published

faqs.question: What is Bitrise used for?
faqs.answer: Bitrise is a CI/CD platform specifically designed to automate building, testing, and deploying iOS and Android mobile apps.

faqs.question: When was Bitrise founded?
faqs.answer: Bitrise was founded in 2014 by Barnabas Birmacher, Viktor Benei, and Daniel Balla.

faqs.question: Where is Bitrise headquartered?
faqs.answer: Bitrise is headquartered in Budapest, Hungary, and also maintains a US office in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Does Bitrise have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Hobby plan is free and includes one private app, limited concurrency, and a monthly credit allotment.

faqs.question: How much do Bitrise paid plans cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start with Starter at roughly 89 to 99 dollars per month, followed by Pro at roughly 200 to 218 dollars per month, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Does Bitrise support both iOS and Android?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides build machines and workflow steps for both iOS (macOS machines) and Android (Linux machines) development.

faqs.question: Does Bitrise offer self-hosted or enterprise deployment?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Enterprise tier offers dedicated and private cloud options, including a self-hosted AWS deployment capability.

faqs.question: Are add-ons like Build Cache and Release Management included in the base price?
faqs.answer: No, advanced Build Cache and Release Management tiers are priced as separate add-ons on top of the base plan.

use_cases.title: Automating Mobile App Builds
